notes.note: Cicero quotes the Greek phrase komikos martys (comic-stage witness), a stock figure from New Comedy who testifies to the obvious; the joke is that Phania's corroboration of Cicero's loyalty to Appius is so redundant it belongs in farce.

notes.anchor: my own colleague besides, and, in the very prestige and learning of our college, a partisan of mine
notes.note: Cicero and Appius were both augurs, members of the prestigious college of augurs; Appius had supported Cicero's augural positions. The collegiality deepened their formal reconciliation after Appius's hostile governorship of Cilicia.

notes.anchor: the recklessness of our friend Dolabella
notes.note: P. Cornelius Dolabella had by this point become engaged to Cicero's daughter Tullia; the 'recklessness' likely refers to his turbulent personal conduct or an early legal scrape, details of which are lost.

Caelius had used the word veternus (cattle-sleep, torpor) for the political stagnation at Rome; Cicero relishes the metaphor and plays on it — the body politic had gone dormant rather than reaching crisis.

The news that C. Scribonius Curio, tribune of the plebs 50 BC, had switched from opposition to active advocacy for Caesar's command was the political sensation of the year; Cicero claims, with characteristic irony, that he had foreseen it.

notes.anchor: kept the arrears of the previous five-year cycle for the tax-farmers without one complaint from our allies
notes.note: The publicani (tax-farmers) collected revenue in five-year cycles (lustra); Cicero as governor of Cilicia had managed the delicate balance between their financial claims and the burden on the provincial subject communities (socii), a notoriously difficult task.
